metadata.title: Speaking Volumes: Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
authors: A L I N N E

abstract:
Many theoretical conclusions core to the study of international politics rely on having access to, and understanding, the rhetoric of international actors. One important development in advancing the empirical study of international relations (IR) theory, therefore, is the availability of machine-analyzable speech data. A collection of fine-grained textual representations of states' speeches in the context of an important international organization, such as the United Nations General Assembly (UNGA), is needed to understand the ideas, preferences, and values that states put forth in their statements. An especially promising use for such a corpus of texts would be to measure states' preferences based on their statements. In an effort to add to the burgeoning field of text-as-data in IR, I present the UNGA Speech Corpus, a collection of over 34,000 speeches delivered by states in the UNGA from 1993 to 2018. I use it to improve on recent work that links text to preferences in IR by combining a structural topic model with locally trained word embeddings to estimate the policy positions of states on specific topics. I then show how these "topic scores" can help scholars to improve their analyses of exigent international issues, such as global climate change governance.

Muchas de las conclusiones teóricas centrales en el estudio de política internacional radican en tener acceso a la retórica de los actores internacionales y comprenderla. Por lo tanto, un avance importante en el estudio empírico de la teoría de las RRII es la disponibilidad de datos discursivos analizables por máquinas. Hace falta un conjunto de representaciones textuales refinadas de los discursos estatales en el contexto de una organización internacional importante, como la Asamblea General de las Naciones Unidas (AGNU), para comprender las ideas, las preferencias y los valores que los estados presentan en sus declaraciones. Un uso especialmente prometedor de dicho corpus de textos podría ser el de medir las preferencias de los Estados en función de sus declaraciones. En un esfuerzo por aportar al prolífero campo de textos como datos en las RRII, presento el Corpus de Discursos de la AGNU, un conjunto de más de 34 000 discursos pronunciados por los Estados en la AGNU desde 1993 hasta 2018. Lo utilizo para mejorar trabajos recientes que vinculan textos con preferencias en las RRII mediante la combinación de un modelo estructural temático con la incorporación de palabras entrenadas localmente para valorar las posiciones políticas de los Estados en determinados temas. Luego, muestro cómo estos puntajes temáticos pueden ayudar a los académicos a mejorar sus análisis de cuestiones internacionales delicadas, como la gobernanza del cambio climático global.

De nombreuses conclusions théoriques au coeur de l'étude de la politique internationale reposent sur l'accès à la rhétorique des acteurs internationaux et sur sa compréhension. La disponibilité de données sur les discours analysables par machine constitue donc une évolution importante dans le progrès de l'étude empirique de la théorie des RI. Une collection de représentations textuelles détaillées des discours des États dans le contexte d'une organisation internationale importante, telle que l'Assemblée générale des Nations unies (AGNU), est nécessaire pour comprendre les idées, les préférences et les valeurs que les États mettent en avant dans leurs déclarations. Une utilisation particulièrement prometteuse d'un tel corpus de textes serait de mesurer les préférences des États d'après leurs États. Dans un effort d'enrichir le domaine du texte en tant que données en plein essor en RI, je présente le Corpus des discours à l'AGNU (UNGA Speech Corpus), une collection de plus de 34 000 discours prononcés par des États à l'AGNU entre 1993 et 2018. Je l'utilise pour apporter une amélioration aux travaux récents qui associent texte et préférences en RI en combinant un modèle thématique structurel avec des intégrations de mots entraînées localement pour estimer les positions politiques des États sur des thèmes spécifiques. Je montre ensuite comment ces scores thématiques peuvent aider les chercheurs à améliorer leurs analyses des questions internationales urgentes, telles que la gouvernance du changement climatique mondial.

sections.paper_id: Turco - 2023 - Speaking Volumes Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
paragraphs: The international realm is characterized by constant comment. States make speeches on foreign policy and global events, both publicly and privately, to domestic and inter-Linnea Turco is an Assistant Instructional Professor in the Committee on International Relations at the University of Chicago.

Author's note : The manuscript and data have benefitted from the generous feedback of colleagues. I am particularly indebted to Alex Thompson, Caleb Pomeroy, and William Minozzi, as well as participants in the Research in International Politics Workshop at the Ohio State University. The data underlying this article are available on the ISQ Dataverse at https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/isq . national audiences alike. 1 The statements of international actors, more generally, are critically important to the study of world politics. After all, besides their behavior, speech is the only other observable activity by which we can study states. There are numerous relevant sources of speech data in the wider realm of international politics, including pronouncements on foreign policy that convey intentions and ideas, internal government documents that detail interna-tional policy agendas, deliberations on treaty and resolution texts that reveal the priorities of groups of actors, and delegates' statements in the context of international organizations (IOs).foot_2 Furthermore, many theoretical conclusions core to the field of international relations (IR) rely on having access to and understanding the rhetoric of international actors. Yet, while we may know a considerable amount theoretically about the importance of speech in global politics, we are only beginning to have a comprehensive empirical understanding of the immense number of words that are generated by its conduct. Additional collections of text data could be usefully employed to continue the theoretical and empirical work of scholars who locate meaningful political concepts in international discourse. This paper advances that project by presenting a new dataset of texts, the United Nations General Assembly (UNGA) Speech Corpus, and uses it to estimate a novel measure of states' preferences on specific international topics.

Text-as-data techniques have become popular in the study of political science ( Grimmer and Stewart 2013 ), and in IR scholars have increasingly used these methods to consider questions related to IOs and foreign policy. Several initiatives have collected text data specifically focused on the United Nations. These include the UN General Debate (UNGD) Corpus ( Baturo, Dasandi, and Mikhaylov 2017 ), spaceTexts, a collection of speeches generated by the United Nations Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space ( Pomeroy 2017 ), and the UN Security Council Debates Corpus ( Schönfeld et al. 2019 ). 3 When it comes to these text data, one critical gap is the lack of fine-grained textual representation of states' speeches in the context of the UNGA. An especially promising application for these data would be to measure states' preferences on particular issues based on their statements. For instance, what are states' preferences on climate change? How similar are the United States and small island developing states on this topic? We cannot fully answer these questions without a text-based measure of states' preferences.

In this paper, I present a new dataset of speeches delivered by states in the United Nations General Assembly from 1993 to 2018. The unit of analysis, individual speech contributions of states in UNGA meetings, can be analyzed with qualitative and quantitative text-as-data methods. In total, the dataset includes over 34,000 individual speech contributions made by member states over this time period. I use the UNGA Speech Corpus here to build on work that has derived states' preferences from texts, and extend it by developing a new method that combines preference scores with topic proportions. This paper makes several important contributions. First, by introducing a comprehensive dataset of country speeches in the United Nations General Assembly, it joins existing and ongoing efforts to advance text-as-data research on political texts in IR. Second, the UNGA Speech Corpus provides new insight into how states speak in the United Nations in "everyday" contexts, going beyond other corpora that include only high-level debate speeches, which represent a fraction of the total speeches states make in the UNGA. Third, the paper offers a new measure of states' preferences on specific international issues. This measure builds on other preference measures anchored in texts and rollcall voting patterns alike ( Voeten 2012 ).

The data and methods presented in what follows are especially relevant to scholars who prioritize discourse as an important source of knowledge about the global political realm ( Fierke 2002 ;Adler 2013 ;Holzscheiter 2014 ). Access to the texts generated by states is central to understanding their identities, which are "constructed by discursive practices and constituted by socially shared meaning-structures" ( Holzscheiter 2014 , 4), and from which preferences flow ( Wendt 1995 ;Guzzini 2000 ). The preference measure proposed in this paper accords with the constructivist understanding of states' interests as intersubjective products of global institutions, and is amenable to analyses that would probe deeper into social processes to understand how preferences on specific international issue domains are formed and evolve over time. Methodologically, while the gold standard for interpreting meaning from text is human analysis, many social scientists have adopted text-as-data methods, considering them complementary to traditional discourse and content analysis ( Bennett 2015 ;McCourt 2019 ;Nelson 2020 ). 4 This paper offers the UNGA Speech Corpus and the methods presented herein as a resource for IR scholars who investigate questions for which the primary evidence is international discourse. In particular, researchers can make use of the UNGA Speech Corpus if they seek to describe the values and ideas that states express and prioritize in their statements to a global audience. For instance, they may use it to weigh in on the decades-old debate on the nuclear taboo ( Tannenwald 1999 ) by mapping whether states' speeches on nuclear weapons in the UNGA are proor anti-taboo. The UNGA Speech Corpus can serve as a useful source of data for scholars seeking empirical evidence for new theoretical concepts, such as the politics of "aspiration talk" and the ways in which international actors use aspirational statements to facilitate agreement and mobilize support ( Finnemore and Jurkovich 2020 , 762). The UNGA Speech Corpus can also be used to understand how states' speeches reflect shifts in global context. For example, if the international order is shifting away from a "liberal" model, scholars might, as Adler and Drieschova (2020) suggest in their proposed framework of "truth subversion," find discursive artifacts of this shift in UNGA speeches.

This paper illustrates the descriptive and inferential utility of the UNGA Speech Corpus with an application to global climate governance. In doing so, it asks the following question: What is the relationship between a state's expressed preferences on climate change and its pursuit of international climate policies? Despite the urgency of the global environmental crisis, quantitative data on international climate policy is scarce ( Bernauer 1995 ;Sprinz 2004 ). Moreover, although states' preferences are crucial to many studies of global climate governance, they are notoriously difficult to quantify. As Keohane and Victor (2016) note, "empirical research on national preferences has been plagued by the fact that governments often avoid making their real preferences clear" (572). Data traditionally used to measure states' preferences, such as roll-call votes on UNGA resolutions, are not available on the topic of climate change. Indeed, as Green and Colgan (2013) argue, a preference measure derived from votes "is not necessarily a good measure of environmental preferences because the UN votes tend to be dominated by geopolitical issues such as the The paper proceeds as follows. First, I present the UNGA Speech Corpus and accompanying details on its composition. The following section introduces descriptive statistics of the corpus in the form of a structural topic model (STM) and word embeddings, illustrating the rich textual resource that the UNGA Speech Corpus offers to scholars of international politics. Next, to further demonstrate the descriptive and inferential utility of the UNGA Speech Corpus, I develop a measure of similarity between states on the issues they discuss. I show how this measure-topic scores-can improve existing explanatory analyses of transnational climate governance and conclude the paper with suggestions for future research.

sections.paper_id: Turco - 2023 - Speaking Volumes Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
paragraphs: The UNGA Speech Corpus is a collection of individual speeches delivered in the UNGA from 1993 to 2018. 5 The time interval, in addition to reflecting the availability of machine-readable documents, is intended to capture international discourse after the Cold War. 6 Overall, the corpus contains the speeches of 212 countries. Table 1 provides an overview of the UNGA Speech Corpus. It shows the number of tokens (individual words) in each individual speech for every year in the corpus. The dataset only includes speeches, as opposed to other General Assembly documents, such as resolutions. Each document is presented in plain-text format, in which every text is one speech given by a single state's representative to the UN. The UNGA Speech Corpus consists of 34,058 documents (speeches). On average, there are 1,228 words in each speech and 77,279 words spoken per state. The mean number of words spoken per year is 454,893.

A key advantage of the UNGA Speech Corpus and this dataset is that there are multiple country-year speech observations. As the documents were scraped and converted into single-speech text files, metadata corresponding to country, year, meeting, and session were also collected for each speech. The corpus was then pre-processed for analysis using standard text cleaning tools that remove superfluous words, numbers, characters, and punctuation ( Welbers, Van Atteveldt, and Benoit 2017 , 251). The UNGA Speech Corpus is beneficial for constructing a measure of states' preferences precisely because it includes speeches from the full UNGA sessions from 1993 to 2018.

Moreover, the large number of speeches in the UNGA Speech Corpus ensures both internal and external validity. A greater number of speeches increases internal validity insofar as the analyst can be more confident that measures based on speeches capture true meaning given that there are more speeches from which to draw. The number of speeches in the UNGA Speech Corpus maximizes external validity such that we can be confident that the preferences measured using these speeches correspond to preferences in the "real world." In the UNGA Speech Corpus, states have many opportunities to speak during sessions that occur throughout the annual meeting. A state may be required to opine during these sessions on themes that would not necessarily have been self-selected for the topic of a formal speech by a head of state in the context of, for instance, the General Debate. In what follows, I leverage this semantic validity to create word vectors that can be used to study texts that are specific to international politics but are too small to generate their own valid word vectors.

As the documents were scraped and converted into single-speech text files, metadata corresponding to country, year, meeting, and session were also collected for each speech. The corpus was then pre-processed for analysis using standard text cleaning tools that remove superfluous words, numbers, characters, and punctuation ( Welbers, Van Atteveldt, and Benoit 2017 , 251). The UNGA Speech Corpus is beneficial for constructing a measure of states' preferences precisely because it includes speeches from the full UNGA sessions from 1993 to 2018.

Moreover, the large number of speeches in the UNGA Speech Corpus ensures both internal and external validity. A greater number of speeches increases internal validity insofar as the analyst can be more confident that measures based on speeches capture true meaning given that there are more speeches from which to draw. The number of speeches in the UNGA Speech Corpus maximizes external validity such that we can be confident that the preferences measured using these speeches correspond to preferences in the "real world." In the UNGA Speech Corpus, states have many opportunities to speak during sessions that occur throughout the annual meeting. A state may be required to opine during these sessions on themes that would not necessarily have been self-selected for the topic of a formal speech by a head of state in the context of, for instance, the General Debate. In what follows, I leverage this semantic validity to create word vectors that can be used to study texts that are specific to international politics but are too small to generate their own valid word vectors.
sections.preview_head: The UNGA Speech Corpus is a collection of individual speeches delivered in the UNGA from 1993 to 2018. 5 The time interval, in addition to reflecting the availability of machine-readable documents, is intended to capture international discourse after the Cold War. 6 Overall, the corpus contains the speeches of 212 countries. Table 1 provides an overview of the UNGA Speech Corpus. It shows the number of tokens (individual words) in each individual speech for every year in the corpus.

sections.header_raw: Descriptive Statistics of the UNGA Speech Corpus
sections.header_norm: descriptive statistics of the unga speech corpus
paragraphs: This section lays out two contributions that the UNGA Speech Corpus offers to scholars of international politics. I follow recent innovations in the use of text-as-data to extract meaning from large, unstructured collections of political documents in order to (1) generate a list of topics within the UNGA Speech Corpus and (2) train word embeddings on the UNGA Speech Corpus. These analyses show that the data contain a rich range of topics that cover a large scope of world politics, and reveal a more valid and accurate embedding space than is presently possible to achieve with current corpora. Besides their contributions separately as descriptive measures, they can be combined to understand states' preferences on specific topics, as I do in a final application using the data.

I use an STM, which considers documents to be the result of some distribution of topics that are composed of a set of words ( Laver, Benoit, and Garry 2003 ;Grimmer 2013 ;Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016 ). STM estimates the distribution of topics as they correspond with metadata; in the case of the UNGA Speech Corpus, this equates to country and year. STMs require a user-specified number of topics ( Roberts et al. 2014 ). To determine the appropriate number of topics, I use the searchK function to run models between 10 and 150 topics, ultimately finding that the ideal number of topics was 139 based on an average of four measures of variance. 7 I use a split-sample design to validate the topics, a step that ensures the model accurately captures and does not overfit the data ( Egami et al. 2018 ). Topic proportions, as well as descriptive labels of the topics and associated identifying words, are reported in Online Appendix Table C.1. 8 Figure 1 shows the top thirty topics by prevalence in the UNGA Speech Corpus and the top words that contribute to each topic. 9

7 Diagnostic plots can be found in Online Appendix Figures B.3b and B.3a. 8 I elaborate further on topic labeling in the Online Appendix. 9 To better understand the contribution of the UNGA Speech Corpus, I compare the results of the STM estimated on the UNGA Speech Corpus with the results of a similar STM estimated on the UNGD Corpus. I focus on comparing the UNGA Speech Corpus to the UNGD Corpus due to their similarities: Both corpora are comprised of speeches delivered in the UNGA, and both contain General Debate speeches. Online Appendix Table D.2 shows that there are a fewer number of topics in the UNGD Corpus than in the UNGA Speech Corpus. Documents with high proportions of the topics identified by the STM can be studied in conjunction with their metadata (country and year) to understand patterns in the UNGA Speech Corpus. Scholars can use textual references to Topic 67, the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change , for instance, to understand states' views on global climate change governance and how preferences on this topic have evolved over time. For example, the island state of Tuvalu, in a speech that contained a high proportion of Topic 67, said: "Climate change is certainly the most serious threat to global security and the survival of humankind. In particular, it is an issue of enormous concern to a highly vulnerable small island state and low-lying atoll nations such as Tuvalu, which resides right at the edge of existence" (A/63/PV.85, 2009). On the same topic, the United States linked the urgency of supporting climate change initiatives with development and justice: "global climate change poses serious challenges to our planet and requires an urgent response and the widest possible cooperation by all countries, as well as intensified efforts by the United Nations system. The United States reaffirms its commitment to leadership in this global endeavour " (A/69/PV.92, 2014). The topics discovered in the UNGA Speech Corpus are also amenable to a number of methodological approaches that go beyond quantitative text-as-data techniques such as historical discourse analysis or process tracing. Results of the STM, released along with the UNGA Speech Corpus, can be used to easily locate speeches on these topics for close-textual analysis.

sections.paper_id: Turco - 2023 - Speaking Volumes Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
sections.header_raw: Word Embeddings in the UNGA Speech Corpus
sections.header_norm: word embeddings in the unga speech corpus
paragraphs: A second contribution that the UNGA Speech Corpus offers to the study of international relations is a large number of documents on which to train word vectors using a word embeddings model ( Turney and Pantel 2010 ;Mikolov et al. 2013 ). Word embeddings are increasingly used by political scientists ( Nay 2017 ;Rudkowsky et al. 2018 ;Jerzak, King, and Strezhnev 2022 ; Rheault and Chochrane 2020 ; Rodman 2020 ; Spirling and Rodriguez 2022 ), but the method has not yet been widely adopted by IR scholars. 10 Embeddings themselves can be studied as objects that measure word usage and meaning, that is, human semantics. And as Spirling and Rodriguez note, "word usage … is of direct and profound interest to political scientists" ( 2022 , 3). These attributes make word embeddings especially attractive to scholars who resist the bag-of-words approach to political text analysis research because it assumes texts and words to be independent of each other ( Hansen 2013 ), cannot adequately provide contextualized inference ( Yarhi-Milo 2014 , 275), and is unable to "proceed inductively" ( Allan 2016 , 29). In fact, the process of embedding speeches (or other texts) into vector space assumes that words and texts are not independent, deriving a contextualized, quantitative representation of words inductively from their position in the text. For IR scholars who are epistemologically opposed to the notion of automated text analysis on the grounds that it does violence to semantic meaning, word embeddings can provide a middle ground. 11 And as IR scholars continue to use word embeddings to study political texts relevant to international politics, the UNGA Speech Corpus can provide accurate word vectors for specifically international questions. 12 Word embeddings are a method of representing words in mathematical terms that preserve the semantic meaning of the words. The method is premised on the idea that words that occur in similar contexts within a corpus tend to have similar meanings ( Turney and Pantel 2010 ). Vectors reflect the semantic relationships between words, and the distance between vectors corresponds to the semantic similarity between the words (and meanings) they represent for the corpus on which they were built ( Spirling and Rodriguez 2022 ). Thus, words and phrases whose meanings are closely related, such as "climate" and "environment," are located close together in terms of vector space. 13 I locally train word embeddings on the UNGA Speech Corpus using the Global Vectors for Word Representation (GloVe) model ( Pennington, Socher, and Manning 2014 ). GloVe learns vectors based on how frequently words cooccur within the entire corpus of texts and words. 14 After 10 A notable exception is Pomeroy, Dasandi, and Mikhaylov (2019) . 11 The full discursive context of any text is not possible to capture entirely in this way, just as the quantification of other kinds of data (such as policy positions) elides information about the social structures that generate those data. Scholars who adhere to a "thick" discursive understanding of social ontology may therefore find it difficult to accept the full validity of word embeddings as this method (and other text-as-data techniques) do not "uncover how language contributes to the construction of reality" ( Pashakhanlou 2017 , 452).

12 Scholars have the option of using pre-trained word embeddings, which use massive text data dumps from sites like Wikipedia and Twitter to estimate the relationships between words. The worry for scholars of political science, and IR more specifically, is that these corpora, and thus the pre-trained word vectors, are not sufficiently similar to text data and semantic structures that are particular to international politics. 13 A commonly invoked example is using embeddings for analogical reasoning such as "king-man + woman = queen," where each word in the equation is the vector that represents it. This example can be interpreted as "king is to man as woman is to queen" ( Mikolov et al. 2013 ).
14 Another popular model is Word2Vec ( Mikolov et al. 2013 ), which predicts target words or contexts by looking word-by-word through the corpus.

standard minimal preprocessing (removing non-text characters and converting to lowercase), I subset the vocabularies to words that appear more than five times in the entire corpus and in at least one percent of documents. This yields a vocabulary of 4,881 words. The key parameters for the model are a window of six and embedding dimensions of 300. 15Best practices for validating and evaluating word embeddings models are still evolving in political science ( Rodman 2020 ;Spirling and Rodriguez 2022 ). Standard practice, however, assesses embeddings by how well they capture the "true" relationships between words ( Mikolov et al. 2013 ). I examine the quality of the embeddings by considering the analogies that are produced by the embeddings model (using the "king-man + woman = queen" style of arithmetic). In other words, do we receive reasonable combinations of words when we query keywords from the word vectors trained on the UNGA Speech Corpus? Here, I present five analogical examples from the embeddings:

The numbers next to the final words give the cosine similarity between the added or subtracted previous three word vectors. One can read the analogies as, for example, kyoto is to climate as rome is to icc , or colonialism is to north as south is to domination . A second contribution that the UNGA Speech Corpus offers to the study of international relations is a large number of documents on which to train word vectors using a word embeddings model ( Turney and Pantel 2010 ;Mikolov et al. 2013 ). Word embeddings are increasingly used by political scientists ( Nay 2017 ;Rudkowsky et al. 2018 ;Jerzak, King, and Strezhnev 2022 ; Rheault and Chochrane 2020 ; Rodman 2020 ; Spirling and Rodriguez 2022 ), but the method has not yet been widely adopted by IR scholars. 10 Embeddings themselves can be studied as objects that measure word usage and meaning, that is, human semantics. And as Spirling and Rodriguez note, "word usage … is of direct and profound interest to political scientists" ( 2022 , 3). These attributes make word embeddings especially attractive to scholars who resist the bag-of-words approach to political text analysis research because it assumes texts and words to be independent of each other ( Hansen 2013 ), cannot adequately provide contextualized inference ( Yarhi-Milo 2014 , 275), and is unable to "proceed inductively" ( Allan 2016 , 29). In fact, the process of embedding speeches (or other texts) into vector space assumes that words and texts are not independent, deriving a contextualized, quantitative representation of words inductively from their position in the text. For IR scholars who are epistemologically opposed to the notion of automated text analysis on the grounds that it does violence to semantic meaning, word embeddings can provide a middle ground. 11 And as IR scholars continue to use word embeddings to study political texts relevant to international politics, the UNGA Speech Corpus can provide accurate word vectors for specifically international questions. 12 Word embeddings are a method of representing words in mathematical terms that preserve the semantic meaning of the words. The method is premised on the idea that words that occur in similar contexts within a corpus tend to have similar meanings ( Turney and Pantel 2010 ). Vectors reflect the semantic relationships between words, and the distance between vectors corresponds to the semantic similarity between the words (and meanings) they represent for the corpus on which they were built ( Spirling and Rodriguez 2022 ). Thus, words and phrases whose meanings are closely related, such as "climate" and "environment," are located close together in terms of vector space. 13 I locally train word embeddings on the UNGA Speech Corpus using the Global Vectors for Word Representation (GloVe) model ( Pennington, Socher, and Manning 2014 ). GloVe learns vectors based on how frequently words cooccur within the entire corpus of texts and words. 14 After 10 A notable exception is Pomeroy, Dasandi, and Mikhaylov (2019) . 11 The full discursive context of any text is not possible to capture entirely in this way, just as the quantification of other kinds of data (such as policy positions) elides information about the social structures that generate those data. Scholars who adhere to a "thick" discursive understanding of social ontology may therefore find it difficult to accept the full validity of word embeddings as this method (and other text-as-data techniques) do not "uncover how language contributes to the construction of reality" ( Pashakhanlou 2017 , 452).

12 Scholars have the option of using pre-trained word embeddings, which use massive text data dumps from sites like Wikipedia and Twitter to estimate the relationships between words. The worry for scholars of political science, and IR more specifically, is that these corpora, and thus the pre-trained word vectors, are not sufficiently similar to text data and semantic structures that are particular to international politics. 13 A commonly invoked example is using embeddings for analogical reasoning such as "king-man + woman = queen," where each word in the equation is the vector that represents it. This example can be interpreted as "king is to man as woman is to queen" ( Mikolov et al. 2013 ).

14 Another popular model is Word2Vec ( Mikolov et al. 2013 ), which predicts target words or contexts by looking word-by-word through the corpus.

standard minimal preprocessing (removing non-text characters and converting to lowercase), I subset the vocabularies to words that appear more than five times in the entire corpus and in at least one percent of documents. This yields a vocabulary of 4,881 words. The key parameters for the model are a window of six and embedding dimensions of 300. 15Best practices for validating and evaluating word embeddings models are still evolving in political science ( Rodman 2020 ;Spirling and Rodriguez 2022 ). Standard practice, however, assesses embeddings by how well they capture the "true" relationships between words ( Mikolov et al. 2013 ). I examine the quality of the embeddings by considering the analogies that are produced by the embeddings model (using the "king-man + woman = queen" style of arithmetic). sections.paper_id: Turco - 2023 - Speaking Volumes Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
sections.header_raw: Estimating Topic Pr efer ences from Text
sections.header_norm: estimating topic pr efer ences from text
paragraphs: Descriptively, the UNGA Speech Corpus covers a large range of topics and produces valid word embeddings. I take these findings a step further by combining the topics generated by the STM with word embeddings to estimate states' preferences on key themes in the corpus, which correspond to important issue domains in world politics. In other words, I calculate the topic preferences of countries using the words in a country's speeches on that topic. 19 In this section, I lay out a methodology to estimate this measure, topic scores , and show the results. How are states' speeches made in the UNGA related to their foreign policy preferences? An established body of literature in political science has used political texts to create measures of actors' preferences. Many of these rely on the use of scaling techniques, in which documents are placed on a spectrum of, for example, ideology or party identification ( Lauderdale and Clark 2014 ;Perry and Benoit 2017 ;Kim, Londregan, and Ratkovic 2018 ). Other research has employed sentiment analysis, a technique that measures the positive or negative valence latent within a text, to gauge political actors' attitudes ( Young and Soroka 2012 ;Ceron et al. 2014 ). The standard for measuring states' foreign policy preferences in IR, meanwhile, is roll-call voting data on UNGA resolutions ( Voeten 2012 ), which has recently been used to estimate ideal points ( Gartzke and Jo 2006 ;Bailey, Strezhnev, and Voeten 2017 ) that capture states' positions with regard to the US-led liberal order.

However, preference estimates based on votes alone are incomplete measures. To begin, votes may be biased, since states often vote in regional or affinity blocs, or cast their votes ceremonially, regardless of their "true" preferences. Votes taken on resolutions in the UNGA are limited to a predetermined agenda of items, but there are many more globally urgent themes about which states speak in day-to-day UNGA meetings. Moreover, vote-based measures rely on a limited range of issues that appear in UNGA resolutions that actually receive a vote (as opposed to those that are adopted without a vote) ( Voeten 2012 ;Häge and Hug 2016 ). In contrast, the text that political actors generate in speeches is a good opportunity for them to "express their contrary and divergent preferences" ( Perry and Benoit 2017 , 2). A text-based measure of states' preferences can complement 19 I thank an anonymous reviewer for this suggested wording. a vote-based scale like ideal points ( Pomeroy, Dasandi, and Mikhaylov 2019 ).

The foregoing methods for estimating political actors' preferences in global politics are focused on developing a general preference measure for states across all issue areas. By combining the insights of the STM and word embeddings, I show how the UNGA Speech Corpus offers a way to understand preferences on specific topics , rather than assuming that states' preferences are homogenous across international issues. To do so, I use topic proportions generated by the STM combined with word embeddings to calculate distances between states on each theme within the UNGA Speech Corpus. I assume that the space between texts is indicative of the space between states' policy positions ( Pomeroy, Dansandi, and Mikhaylov 2019 ). I leverage the large number of speeches in the UNGA Speech Corpus, along with the topics estimated using the STM, to measure the distance between documents, and therefore states, on specific topics that correspond to international issues. This approach improves on text scaling techniques in two ways: First, by using word vectors (and the distances between states' speeches on a topic) to determine the poles of the preferences scale for each topic, rather than requiring documents be hand-labeled; and second, by assigning preference scores that are non-binary. A combined STM/word embeddings strategy also improves on other preference measures in IR by offering a topic-specific measure of preferences. In what follows, I lay out the details of this method and use it to measure states' preferences on global climate governance.

sections.paper_id: Turco - 2023 - Speaking Volumes Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
paragraphs: The topics identified by the STM reveal information about the substance of each document and correspond to key political issue domains. Topic proportions identify whether and how much the content of any one speech is generative of a particular topic. I use this information to determine countries' preferences vis-à-vis each respective topic by subsetting the corpus to documents based on their five most prevalent topics. To derive preferences from states' speeches, I use the word embeddings trained on the full UNGA Speech Corpus to determine the vector space of words within texts aggregated by country, for each topic estimated by the STM. In other words, I concatenate the speeches of every country in the data by topic such that the unit of analysis is one large text observation per countrytopic. 20 This approach allows me to calculate similarities for each country for every topic in the corpus, a method that ultimately generates a measure of countries' preferences on each topic.

To calculate similarities between country-topics, I employ a document-level distance measure, relaxed Word Mover's Distance (RWMD), to the word vectors trained using the GloVe model described above ( Kusner et al. 2015 ;Pomeroy, Dasandi, and Mikhaylov 2019 ). The assumption of RWMD, like that of word embeddings, is that distances between word vectors are semantically meaningful. It treats text documents as weighted collections of embedded words, such that the similarity (or "distance") between two documents is the minimum space that words from one document would need to move to match the embedded words of the second document. 21 When speeches are aggregated by country and topic (and speeches are selected by modal topic proportion), RWMD calculates the distance between countries' speeches on each topic, interpreted as a measure of the distance between their preferences on each topic. 22 After calculating word embeddings and document term matrices for each topic in the UNGA Speech Corpus, I find similarities between each country within that topic sub-data. The similarity measures, or topic scores, generated from the RWMD similarity scores, are normalized on a 0 to 1 scale for interpretability in the plots that follow.

Indeed, we might be interested in understanding states' preferences regarding a specific international debate, event, or phenomenon. These are well represented in the themes within the UNGA Speech Corpus, for instance, the debate on anti-personnel landmines (Topic 82), the War in Iraq (Topic 98), and the global phenomenon of food security (Topic 81). For those scholars who want to understand the trajectory of these global themes, topic scores offer a useful measure of states' preferences on the specific issues they investigate. As mentioned above, the topics estimated with the UNGA Speech Corpus are broader than the themes contained within the UNGD Corpus, an attribute that recommends the UNGA Speech Corpus to this particular task. The method I developed here, which combines the results of a topic model with RWMD in order to estimate actors' preferences, could be used on others like corpora that catalog political speeches given by more than one actor. 23 One might argue that endeavoring to derive a measure of states' preferences from their speeches in the context of the UNGA uses an understanding of words in which simple utterances are assumed to contain true meaning. Topic scores, moreover, could be said to be a poor proxy for the expert each term was required to appear in at least a quarter of the documents in the corpus to be included in the topic score analysis.

21 I use RWMD as opposed to other measures of document similarity, such as cosine similarity or Euclidean distance, because RWMD uses information from word embeddings as well as document term matrices in calculating the similarity between texts. It has the additional advantage of high classification accuracy ( Huang et al. 2016 ). 22 The RWMD approach concatenates countries' speeches across multiple years for a given topic. Since there is a possibility that countries' preferences on that topic may change over time, topic scores estimated with the aggregated speech data may obscure that information. In the context of preferences on climate governance, temporal changes in states' preferences are especially meaningful ( Hovi, Sprinz, and Underdal 2009 ;Victor 2011 ;Urpelainen 2012 ;Colgan 2018 ;Colgan, Green, and Hale 2021 ). 23 In order to further check the validity of the topic scores estimated using the UNGA Speech Corpus, I examine correlations between ideal points (which capture foreign policy preferences in general) and topic scores, grouped by topic. While some topic scores are more positively associated with ideal points, suggesting that states' preferences on certain topics are reflected by ideal points, other topic scores are negatively associated with ideal points, suggesting that states' preferences on these topics are not captured by the ideal points measure. I discuss these correlations more in-depth in the Online Appendix.

knowledge of IR scholars. This critique, which we might call the problem of "cheap talk," may indeed introduce a degree of uncertainty to the text data presented in this paper. Yet despite this, "language is still one of the most reliable means by which humans communicate and articulate their thoughts and preferences" ( Bayaram and Ta 2020 , 188). Other studies have shown convincing evidence that preferences can indeed be deduced from measures of linguistic similarity ( Baturo, Dasandi, and Mikhaylov 2017 ;Pomeroy, Dasandi, and Mikhaylov 2019 ;Gurciullo and Mikhaylov 2017 ). Further, even if publicly uttered words are cheap and do not contain any deeper ideational meaning, they can still have political power which international actors use to, for instance, legitimate their behavior without sufficient domestic deliberation ( Crawford 2002 ;Kornprobst 2019 ), strategically ensnare political opponents ( Schimmelfennig 2001 ;Krebs and Jackson 2007 ), constrain themselves through reputational or structural effects ( Johnson 1993 ;Goddard 2006 ;Brutger and Kertzer 2018 ), or shame others into changing their behavior ( Terman and Voeten 2018 ).

Topic scores, moreover, could be said to be a poor proxy for the expert each term was required to appear in at least a quarter of the documents in the corpus to be included in the topic score analysis.

21 I use RWMD as opposed to other measures of document similarity, such as cosine similarity or Euclidean distance, because RWMD uses information from word embeddings as well as document term matrices in calculating the similarity between texts. sections.paper_id: Turco - 2023 - Speaking Volumes Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
paragraphs: At the outset of this paper, I claimed that different areas of IR scholarship would benefit from a measure of states' preferences vis-à-vis specific topics. Topic scores generated with the UNGA Speech Corpus can offer insight into international issues about which we do not have systematic information on states' preferences, past events about which we would like to understand actors' behavior based on their preferences, and ongoing global issues where states' preferences, or the effects of these preferences, are still unclear.

One such area is climate change. Figure 2 reports states' preferences on the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC), Topic 67. 24 How do we interpret these results? The distance between states corresponds to distance between their preferences. We might note that the distribution of states below and above the 0.5 line is fairly even, as opposed to skewing in one direction. This implies that there is a wide spread of opinions on the UNFCCC. The United States, which has an ambivalent relationship with global climate governance, is positioned in the top third of the data, scoring similarly to Germany and Australia. The preferences of small island developing states, which although they are among the least responsible for climate change are the most likely to suffer from its harmful effects, Papua New Guinea, Trinidad and Tobago, Cuba, and Micronesia are among the lowest. Topic scores could be incorporated into studies on international and transnational climate change governance as a covariate to analyze the factors that explain cooperation on this important international issue. I demonstrate the utility of doing so below. Furthermore, topic scores on the UNFCCC may provide a helpful starting point for theoretical investigations on climate change (e.g., Allan 2017b ).

The UNGA Speech Corpus, and the topic scores generated by it, contributes richer topics and embedding space to the study of IR. It also adds inferentially. To show this, I replicate the negative binomial model estimated by Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) , which considers why states participate in transnational climate change initiatives. The authors ask how national policies on climate change influence participation in transnational climate governance initiatives. They argue that these national policies create incentives for actors other than the state (including those operating within 0.00 0.25 0.50 0.75 1.00 BAH PAL SUD GRC PNG ROM SKN NEP TRI CAP MAC KZK AAB CAM ETH SLU VAN VEN BAR CAN NOR CAO FSM SAL BOL CHA EST HUN MNG POR RVN SLV SNM LIB MAD CHL CUB DJI ITA NAU SAU TAJ UAE BEL URU COS FRN GNB MAW PHI TAZ BHM COM CON ECU EGY EQG PAP SWA CYP LAO LIE MON NIG SWZ GAB UKG BNG BRA HAI LEB NAM NTH PAR SIE STP SWD ZIM GUI KEN LIT RWA TUN ZAM PAN SRI SSD SYR ARG GAM MEX SPN YEM DEN INS NIC SVG WSM MNC RUS CEN UZB BOT GRN MAA SEY ANG AUL BHU IRN ROK ARM BLR KUW MOR SAF GMY GUY USA AUS MSI NIR POL THI JAM SUR BFO MAL TUV ALB GHA ICE NEW AND BEN KIR OMA IND KYR LUX PER SEN CZR JPN GUA BLZ BOS SIN MAS FIJ QAT ALG MZM FIN COL SLO TUR MYA UGA ISR TON CRO LES MLT SOL UKR CHN DMA MLD PAK Country Topic Score Figure 2. States' preferences on the UNFCCC the state) to engage in international efforts for climate governance because doing so enables sub-and non-state actors to fulfill their domestic policy obligations.

The dependent variable in the model is a count of the instances of participation in transnational climate governance across countries. This indicator measures how many cases of participation were observed in the seventy-one initiatives in the year 2012. I include all of the original explanatory variables from the baseline model in this replication. How might topic scores fit the model theoretically? Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) argue that transnational governance initiatives have increased in response to "the stagnation of the intergovernmental regime under the UN-FCCC … as a reaction to the inadequacy of state-based institutions" (254). As states decreased their emphasis on national commitments made in the context of the UNFCCC, initiatives by transnational actors increased to fill the void ( Green 2013 ). Topic scores for Topic 67 measure states' preferences toward climate change governance. We might, therefore, expect them to have a similar effect to the two key explanatory variables included in the authors' model: the Environmental Performance Index (EPI) Climate Policy Indicator, which captures the ambitiousness of a government's climate change policies, and a count of all international environmental agreements (IAE) ratified by a country-together meant to capture domestic environmental policy. In their analyses, Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) find that EPI and IAE ratifications have positive effects on states' participation in transnational climate governance. If topic scores for Topic 67 are a good indicator for national preferences on transnational climate governance, we should expect topic scores to have a similar positive effect on participation in transnational climate governance. Including a measure of climate topic scores in the model with similar effects could serve as a robustness check for the original analyses by Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) .

To illustrate the inferential contribution of topic scores on the UNFCCC, I replicate the baseline results from Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) , using a negative binomial model to estimate transnational climate cooperation as the dependent variable. The results are reported in Table 3 . Column 1 reports the baseline model, which replicates the findings of Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) . I then add topic scores (preferences) on the UNFCCC as a covariate in the model. Column 2 reports the results of the model with the included topic scores. There are several differences between the models that I discuss below in turn. First, while most of the effects, in terms of sign and significance, are the same in the second model, Federalism has a significant negative effect with the inclusion of the topic score variable, indicating that more domestic freedom increases the odds of a state having lower levels of climate gov-ernance by a factor of 47 percent. 25 Second, when climate topic scores are added to the model, the effect of a unit increase in Trade leads to a nearly 100 percent decrease in the odds of a state having climate governance, compared to a 5.8 percent decrease in the odds of climate governance in the baseline model findings. Although it is not significant, this finding suggests that economic interconnectedness predicts lower levels of transnational climate governance initiatives. Third, with the inclusion of the climate topic scores, the effect of INGO network membership becomes positive and significant, a 90 percent difference in the odds of climate governance for a one-unit increase in INGO membership. This finding confirms that of Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) in their second model specification.

Finally, I find that states' preferences on the UNFCCC have a significant positive relationship with transnational climate governance. We can interpret the coefficient as a unit increase in topic score leads to a 141 percent increase in the odds of a country having transnational climate governance. 26 Using the position of the United States in Figure 2 for orientation, when a country's preferences move closer the United States (that is, they increase), it is more likely to cooperate on global climate governance. Andonova, Hale, and Roger (2017) find that national policies incentivize transnational governance; the addition of topic scores complements their analyses by providing further evidence that country-specific support for the international climate change regime increases instances of transnational governance. Table 3 also reports measures of model fit. Adjusted R 2 is improved in the model that includes topic scores on the UNFCCC. Future studies may incorporate topic scores on the full range of topics contained within the UNGA Speech Corpus as covariates into other causal or explanatory statistical models to the same effect.

sections.paper_id: Turco - 2023 - Speaking Volumes Introducing the UNGA Speech Corpus
paragraphs: The UNGA is a forum in which ever y countr y in the world voices their opinions and deliberates with other states on crucial (and sometimes trivial) international political issues. In this paper, I have presented a new collection of these speeches, the UNGA Speech Corpus. The UNGA Speech Corpus is a rich resource that can be added to the growing list of collections of text data used for the study of international politics. It can be analyzed through qualitative or quantitative text-as-data methods, and as I have demonstrated above, it contributes both descriptive and inferential resources to the study of international politics.

The use of text data to study international politics is in its beginning stages. There are many theoretical pathways that these data can be used to navigate moving forward, and indeed, different corpora are suited to specific research tasks. When choosing an appropriate corpus of speeches to study international politics, researchers should consider the following questions. First, does the scope of the research question pertain to cross-national phenomena or strictly to one state's foreign policy? This is of consequence depending on whether the researcher requires a corpus that contains the statements of many states or the speeches of a single key state. Second, who are the actors, what is the time period and topics relevant to the research question, and does the corpus capture these adequately? In other words, does the corpus contain the desired information? Finally, if quantitative methods are to be used, is there a sufficient number of texts from which to estimate meaningful relationships between words? This consideration pertains to classification and word embeddings, insofar as the models require enough data to accurately model semantic structures within texts.

The UNGA Speech Corpus has two advantages that correspond to these queries, both substantive and methodological: First, it contains a nuanced range of topics that capture specific historical events as well as general global issues. These topics may interest scholars conducting quantitative or qualitative research on states in IOs. Additionally, the field of IR does not yet have a source of locally trained word embeddings that can accurately capture the semantic structures found in texts pertaining to international politics. The pre-trained word vectors on the UNGA Speech Corpus that I present here are a resource for any IR scholar working with corpora that are too small on which to train word vectors.

In this paper, I use an STM and word embeddings to generate topic scores, a measure of states' preferences on 139 textual themes discovered within the UNGA Speech Corpus. Topic scores present a measure of preferences that goes beyond voting data, and captures states' positions on specific topics rather than differences between foreign policies in general. They can be incorporated by scholars interested in a number of diverse research agendas within the field of international relations, with the potential to enrich our understanding of states' views on significant global topics. In the context of global climate governance, I show how topic scores can enrich our descriptive and inferential understanding of states' support for the UNFCCC. Future research using the UNGA Speech Corpus and topic scores may look further into disaggregating scores by year, as well as applying these data to theoretical and empirical models of global politics.
